mission.title: Install and Configure Perses on Kubernetes
mission.description: Perses is a CNCF sandbox project that provides a dashboard tool for visualizing observability data from various sources like Prometheus, Tempo, Loki, and Pyroscope. Installing Perses allows you to create and manage dashboards that integrate multiple observability pillars in one place.

steps.title: Add the Perses Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Perses Helm repository to your Helm client to access the charts.
```bash
helm repo add perses https://perses.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Perses using Helm
steps.description:
Install Perses in a new namespace called 'perses' with the specified version.
```bash
helm install perses perses/perses --namespace perses --create-namespace --version v0.53.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Perses pods are running successfully in the 'perses' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace perses
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access the Perses dashboard
steps.description:
Set up port-forwarding to access the Perses dashboard locally.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/perses --namespace perses 8080:80
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
You may want to configure resource limits for the Perses deployment. Edit the values.yaml file or use Helm to set resource limits.
```bash
helm upgrade perses perses/perses --namespace perses --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Perses will show the pods running in the 'perses' namespace, and you will be able to access the dashboard at http://localhost:8080. You can also confirm that resource limits are applied by checking the deployment configuration.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add perses https://perses.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
helm install perses perses/perses --namespace perses --create-namespace --version v0.53.0
helm upgrade perses perses/perses --namespace perses --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the Perses release from the 'perses' namespace using Helm to remove the deployment and associated resources.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) related to Perses to ensure a clean removal.

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the 'perses' namespace and verify that all resources have been removed.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and any important data related to the Perses installation.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to install the latest version of Perses.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check that the Perses pods are running the new version successfully in the 'perses' namespace.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue indicates that the Perses pods are failing to start. Use the following command to check the logs for more details: 
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace perses
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Perses dashboard, ensure that the port-forwarding is set up correctly. Check the service status with: 
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace perses
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If the pods are not starting due to resource constraints, check the resource requests and limits set in the deployment. You can view the current resource usage with: 
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace perses
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade command fails, check for any breaking changes in the new version. You can view the Helm release history with: 
```bash
helm history perses --namespace perses
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
